quiz_questions.question_text: What causes friction?

options.text: The interaction between the microscopic irregularities (roughness) of two surfaces in contact, along with molecular adhesion
The color and visual appearance of the surface, because darker surfaces generate more friction than lighter-colored surfaces at the molecular interaction level
Air molecules pushing against the surfaces and creating resistance between them, since atmospheric pressure is the primary source of friction between any two objects
Gravity pulling the two objects together with a force proportional to their combined masses, because gravitational attraction is the fundamental cause of all friction

quiz_questions.explanation: Friction arises from the electromagnetic interactions between the microscopic bumps and molecules on contacting surfaces. Even 'smooth' surfaces are rough at the microscopic level.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between static and kinetic friction?

options.text: Static friction only exists on inclined surfaces and not on flat horizontal surfaces, because the angle of the incline is what creates the frictional resistance
Static friction and kinetic friction are exactly the same physical phenomenon described by the same coefficient, with no difference in their maximum force values
Static friction prevents motion from starting; kinetic friction opposes motion that is already occurring. Static friction is generally greater than kinetic friction
Kinetic friction is always greater than static friction for any pair of surfaces, because moving objects experience more resistance than stationary objects do

quiz_questions.explanation: Static friction acts when there is no relative motion (it prevents sliding). Kinetic friction acts during relative motion (sliding). Static friction has a higher maximum value.

quiz_questions.question_text: The formula for the maximum static friction force is:

options.text: f = the coefficient of friction times weight times the contact area, because friction depends on how large the surface area of contact is between the objects
f_s,max = μ_s × N, where μ_s is the coefficient of static friction and N is the normal force
f = ma, where m is the mass and a is the acceleration, since friction is calculated the same way as any other force using Newton's second law of motion
f = the coefficient of friction times mg only, because the normal force is always equal to the weight and contact area determines the actual friction value

quiz_questions.explanation: Maximum static friction depends on the coefficient of static friction (μ_s) and the normal force (N). The contact area does not appear in the formula.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for kinetic friction?

options.text: f_k = μ_k × N, where μ_k is the coefficient of kinetic friction and N is the normal force
f_k = m times g, where the kinetic friction force equals the weight of the object regardless of the coefficient or the nature of the contacting surfaces
f_k = the coefficient of kinetic friction times weight times velocity, because faster-moving objects experience proportionally more kinetic friction force
f_k = the coefficient of kinetic friction times the contact area, since kinetic friction depends primarily on how much surface area is touching between objects

quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic friction = μ_k N. It is approximately constant regardless of the sliding speed (to first approximation).

quiz_questions.question_text: In which direction does friction act?

options.text: Opposite to the direction of motion or attempted motion
In the direction of motion
Toward the center of the Earth
Perpendicular to the surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Friction always opposes the relative motion (or tendency of motion) between surfaces. If the object slides right, friction acts left.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is tension in a rope?

options.text: A pulling force transmitted through the rope from one end to the other
The weight of the rope
The stretching limit of the rope
The friction between the rope and an object

quiz_questions.explanation: Tension is the force transmitted through a rope, string, or cable when pulled taut. In an ideal (massless) rope, tension is the same at every point along it.

quiz_questions.question_text: In an ideal (massless, inextensible) rope, what is true about tension throughout the rope?

options.text: The tension is the same at every point along the rope
Tension increases toward the middle
Tension is zero in the middle
Tension decreases toward the middle

quiz_questions.explanation: For a massless rope, Newton's second law requires that the net force on any segment is zero (since m = 0). This means tension must be constant throughout.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does friction always resist motion?

options.text: Friction never causes any object to accelerate under any conditions, because friction is by definition a decelerating force that always removes kinetic energy
Yes friction always slows things down without exception, because the sole function of friction in every physical situation is to reduce an object's velocity
Friction always opposes relative motion or the tendency of relative motion between surfaces — but it can cause an object to accelerate (e.g., walking)
No friction sometimes accelerates objects in any arbitrary direction regardless of the surface orientation, because friction can point in any direction at all

quiz_questions.explanation: Friction opposes RELATIVE motion between surfaces. When you walk, friction between your foot and the ground acts forward on you, enabling you to accelerate forward.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 10 kg box sits on a surface with μ_s = 0.4. What is the maximum static friction? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 4 N — using f = coefficient times mass gives f = 0.4 times 10 = 4 N, because the friction formula uses mass directly rather than the normal force value
10 N — using f = coefficient times g gives f = 0.4 times 10 divided by 0.4 = 10 N, because the gravity value alone determines the static friction force
100 N — using f = N = mg gives f = 10 times 10 = 100 N, because the maximum static friction force always equals the full normal force on a horizontal surface

quiz_questions.explanation: N = mg = 100 N. f_s,max = μ_s × N = 0.4 × 100 = 40 N. You need more than 40 N of horizontal force to start the box sliding.

quiz_questions.question_text: A box slides across a floor with μ_k = 0.3 and a normal force of 50 N. What is the kinetic friction force?
quiz_questions.explanation: f_k = μ_k × N = 0.3 × 50 = 15 N, opposing the direction of sliding.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes friction?

options.text: A force that only acts on surfaces that are visibly rough to the naked eye, since perfectly smooth surfaces have zero friction between them at all contact points
A non-contact force that operates at a distance between any two objects through their gravitational fields, without requiring any physical contact between surfaces
A force that always prevents all motion completely, stopping any object from moving no matter how large the applied force is relative to the friction coefficient
A contact force that opposes the relative motion or tendency of relative motion between two surfaces, arising from molecular interactions at the contact interface

quiz_questions.explanation: Friction is a surface contact force. It can be static (preventing motion) or kinetic (opposing ongoing motion). It depends on surface properties and normal force.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the coefficient of friction?

options.text: A dimensionless number (μ) that characterizes the frictional properties of a pair of surfaces — higher values mean more friction between those surfaces
The total surface area of contact between the two objects, because larger contact areas produce proportionally more friction between the sliding surfaces
The weight of the heavier surface in the pair, since the more massive object determines the friction level regardless of the properties of the lighter surface
The friction force measured in newtons between the surfaces, calculated by multiplying the mass of the sliding object by the gravitational acceleration constant

quiz_questions.explanation: μ is a property of the surface pair, not a single surface. It has no units. μ_s (static) is typically greater than μ_k (kinetic) for the same surfaces.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes static friction?

options.text: A constant force that always equals exactly the product of the static coefficient times the normal force, regardless of how much applied force is present
Friction that acts only on objects that are completely stationary and can never appear on objects that have been in motion at any point during the experiment
The friction present on very smooth surfaces like polished glass, since the smoothness of the surface determines whether friction is classified as static
The friction force that acts on an object that is not moving relative to the surface it contacts, preventing the onset of sliding — it adjusts from zero up to a maximum value μ_s N

quiz_questions.explanation: Static friction self-adjusts to match the applied force up to its maximum (μ_s N). If you push with 5 N, static friction provides 5 N. Push with 10 N, it provides 10 N — until you exceed f_s,max.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes kinetic friction?

options.text: Friction that only acts on rolling objects like wheels and spheres, and does not apply to objects that slide or translate across flat surfaces without rotating
A friction force that varies proportionally with the contact area between the two surfaces, since larger contact patches generate more kinetic friction force
The approximately constant friction force that acts on an object sliding across a surface, equal to μ_k × N and directed opposite to the sliding motion
Friction that increases proportionally with the speed of the sliding object, because faster objects experience greater resistance from the surface they slide on

quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic friction is approximately constant for a given normal force and surface pair. It acts while the object slides and is usually less than the maximum static friction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the normal force in the context of friction?

options.text: The gravitational force pulling an object downward toward the center of the Earth, which has a magnitude equal to the mass of the object multiplied by g
The perpendicular force that a surface exerts on an object, which determines the maximum friction force through f = μN
A force that acts parallel to the contact surface, sliding along it in the direction of applied force rather than pressing perpendicular into the surface
The coefficient of friction itself, which is a dimensionless number characterizing the surface pair rather than a force with magnitude and direction

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force directly scales friction: f = μN. Any change in normal force (e.g., pushing down on an object, or tilting a surface) changes the friction force proportionally.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes tension force?

options.text: The total weight of an object hanging from a rope or string, since tension is simply another name for the gravitational force acting on suspended masses
The frictional resistance that exists within the internal fibers of a rope, opposing any stretching or deformation of the rope's material structure
A compressive pushing force transmitted through a rigid rod or bar, capable of pushing objects apart rather than pulling them together across distances
A pulling force transmitted through a flexible connector (rope, string, cable, chain) that is taut, always directed along the connector away from the object it pulls

quiz_questions.explanation: Tension pulls on objects at both ends of a connector. It is always a pull (never a push). For an ideal rope, tension is uniform throughout.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes an ideal rope or string?

options.text: A theoretical rope that has zero mass and does not stretch, ensuring that tension is constant throughout its length
A rope that has a standard mass of exactly one kilogram per meter of length, providing a baseline reference value for tension calculations in all problems
A rope that can never break no matter how much force is applied to it, having infinite tensile strength while maintaining normal mass and elasticity properties
A rope that has infinite elasticity and stretches to any length under any applied force, absorbing unlimited energy before returning to its original length

quiz_questions.explanation: An ideal rope is a simplifying assumption where mass = 0 (so tension is uniform) and the rope is inextensible (so connected objects have the same acceleration).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a pulley in the context of tension?

options.text: A device that stores potential energy in its rotating mechanism, converting kinetic energy into stored rotational energy for later release when needed
A wheel that redirects the direction of tension in a rope while (for an ideal massless, frictionless pulley) maintaining constant tension throughout
A device that doubles the tension force in a rope by mechanical advantage, always requiring exactly half the input force to lift any given weight upward
A wheel mechanism whose primary purpose is to create friction in the rope, generating heat and resistance that allow the user to control the rope's speed

quiz_questions.explanation: An ideal pulley changes the direction of the tension force without changing its magnitude. Real pulleys have friction and mass that slightly reduce the transmitted force.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes limiting friction?

options.text: The kinetic friction force that acts on a moving object as it slides across a surface, having a constant value equal to the kinetic coefficient times normal force
The friction present on the smoothest possible surface available, representing the minimum achievable friction for any pair of contacting materials in contact
A state of zero friction between two surfaces, achieved when the surfaces are perfectly polished and no molecular interactions occur at the contact interface
The maximum static friction force (μ_s N) — the threshold force that must be exceeded to initiate sliding of the object

quiz_questions.explanation: Limiting friction is the maximum value of static friction: f_s,max = μ_s N. Below this force, static friction matches the applied force exactly. Above it, the object begins to slide.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes rolling friction?

options.text: The resistive force that opposes rolling motion, caused by deformation at the contact region — it is typically much smaller than sliding friction
Exactly the same physical phenomenon as kinetic friction applied to circular objects, with identical coefficient values and identical mathematical formulas used
Friction that only acts on cylindrical objects like perfect cylinders and has no effect on spherical objects like balls or other non-cylindrical rolling shapes
Friction that causes wheels to spin faster by adding rotational energy to the wheel, increasing the angular velocity rather than opposing the rolling motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Rolling friction arises from the slight deformation of the surfaces at contact. It is much less than sliding friction, which is why wheels and ball bearings are efficient.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 20 kg crate sits on a floor (μ_s = 0.5, μ_k = 0.3). You push horizontally with 80 N. Does the crate move? What is the friction force? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: Yes it moves — 80 N exceeds the maximum friction of 60 N, so the crate begins sliding with kinetic friction providing the only opposing force on the surface
Yes it moves — 80 N of applied force is more than enough to move any crate regardless of mass, surface properties, or the coefficient of friction between them
No — f_s,max = 0.5 × 200 = 100 N. Since your 80 N push is less than 100 N, static friction provides exactly 80 N to balance your push. The crate stays put
No it does not move — friction is always exactly equal to the maximum value of 100 N regardless of the applied force, providing constant resistance at all times

quiz_questions.explanation: N = mg = 200 N. f_s,max = μ_s N = 0.5 × 200 = 100 N. Since the applied 80 N < 100 N, static friction adjusts to exactly 80 N (opposing the push), and the crate does not move.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the same situation (20 kg crate, μ_s = 0.5, μ_k = 0.3), you now push with 120 N. What happens?

options.text: The crate moves but with exactly zero acceleration because kinetic friction automatically adjusts to equal the applied force and maintain constant velocity
The crate accelerates at 6 m/s squared because the net force equals the applied force minus the weight: a = (120 minus 100) divided by 20/6 = 6 m/s squared
The crate does not move because kinetic friction increases to 120 N to match the applied force, preventing any motion from starting on the rough floor surface
The crate accelerates — 120 N > f_s,max (100 N), so it starts sliding. Kinetic friction = 0.3 × 200 = 60 N. a = (120 − 60)/20 = 3 m/s²

quiz_questions.explanation: 120 N > f_s,max = 100 N → sliding begins. f_k = μ_k N = 0.3 × 200 = 60 N. F_net = 120 − 60 = 60 N. a = 60/20 = 3 m/s².

quiz_questions.question_text: A 5 kg mass hangs from a rope attached to the ceiling. What is the tension in the rope? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 25 N — dividing the weight by 2 gives T = mg/2 = 50/2 = 25 N, because a hanging mass only transfers half its weight as tension to the supporting rope above
100 N — doubling the weight gives T = 2mg = 2 times 50 = 100 N, because the rope must support both the mass and the reaction force from the ceiling attachment
50 N — the mass is in equilibrium, so T = mg = 5 × 10 = 50 N
5 N — the tension equals just the mass value T = m = 5 N, because tension in newtons is numerically equal to the mass in kilograms for any hanging object

quiz_questions.explanation: The mass is static. Forces on it: tension T upward, weight mg = 50 N downward. Equilibrium: T = mg = 50 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two blocks (3 kg and 5 kg) are connected by a rope on a frictionless surface. A 24 N force pulls the 5 kg block. What is the acceleration and the tension in the rope between them?

options.text: a = 3 m/s squared and T = 15 N — the tension equals the force times the lighter mass ratio: T = 24 times 3/8 = 9 then adjusted to 15 N for the heavier block
a = 3 m/s², T = 9 N — system: a = 24/(3+5) = 3 m/s²; for the 3 kg block: T = 3 × 3 = 9 N
a = 3 m/s squared and T = 24 N — the full applied force is transmitted through the rope between the blocks, so tension equals the applied force of 24 newtons
a = 4.8 m/s squared and T = 14.4 N — using only the heavier block: a = 24/5 = 4.8, then T = 3 times 4.8 = 14.4 N from the lighter block's perspective

quiz_questions.explanation: System: a = F/(m₁ + m₂) = 24/8 = 3 m/s². For the 3 kg block (only force is tension): T = m₁ × a = 3 × 3 = 9 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: A person pulls a sled (15 kg) with a rope at 30° above horizontal. The rope tension is 60 N. μ_k = 0.2. What is the acceleration? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: a = 0.87 m/s squared — using only the horizontal force component divided by mass: a = 60 cos 30 / 15 = 52/60 = 0.87, ignoring the friction force entirely
Horizontal component = 60cos30° ≈ 52 N. Normal force = 150 − 60sin30° = 150 − 30 = 120 N. f_k = 0.2 × 120 = 24 N. a = (52 − 24)/15 ≈ 1.87 m/s²
a = 4 m/s squared — using the full tension without resolving components: a = 60/15 = 4 m/s squared, because the angle does not affect the net force calculation
a = 2.8 m/s squared — using the vertical component as the driving force: a = 60 sin 30 / 15 = 30/15 = 2, then adding friction gives approximately 2.8 m/s squared

quiz_questions.question_text: An Atwood machine has masses of 4 kg and 6 kg connected by a rope over a frictionless pulley. What is the acceleration and tension? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: a = 5 m/s squared and T = 50 N — using only the heavier mass: a = (6)(10)/(6+4) = 6, adjusted to 5, and T = 10 times 5 = 50 N from the total system weight
a = 10 m/s squared and T = 40 N — using a = g = 10 m/s squared since both masses are in free fall, and T = 4 times 10 = 40 N from the lighter mass's weight
a = 2 m/s squared and T = 60 N — using T = m2 times g = 6 times 10 = 60 N, because the tension equals the full weight of the heavier mass in all Atwood machines

quiz_questions.explanation: a = (m₂ − m₁)g/(m₁ + m₂) = 20/10 = 2 m/s². For the 4 kg mass: T − 4g = 4a → T = 4(10 + 2) = 48 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car's tires have μ_s = 0.8 with the road. The car has mass 1500 kg. What is the maximum braking force before the tires start to skid? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 15000 N — using the full weight as the maximum braking force: f = mg = 1500 times 10 = 15000 N, because maximum friction always equals the total weight of the car
8000 N — using f = coefficient times mass: f = 0.8 times 1500 = 1200 then multiplied by some factor to get approximately 8000 N maximum braking force on the road
1200 N — using f = coefficient times mass directly: f = 0.8 times 1500 = 1200 N, because the friction formula uses mass in kilograms rather than the normal force

quiz_questions.explanation: N = mg = 1500 × 10 = 15000 N. f_s,max = μ_s N = 0.8 × 15000 = 12000 N. Beyond this force, the tires lose grip and start skidding (switching to kinetic friction).

quiz_questions.question_text: You hang a 2 kg weight from the middle of a horizontal rope. The rope sags slightly. Is the tension in the rope greater than, less than, or equal to 20 N?

options.text: Less than 20 N — the rope sags, which means some of the weight is supported by the sag itself, reducing the tension needed in each side of the rope below 10 N each

Greater than 20 N — the two sides of the rope must each have an upward component that together support the weight. Since the rope is nearly horizontal, each side makes a small angle with horizontal, requiring large tension to provide enough vertical component
Exactly 10 N in each side of the rope — the weight divides equally between the two sides, giving exactly half of 20 N = 10 N of tension in each half of the rope
Equal to exactly 20 N — the total tension in the rope must equal the weight of the hanging mass, so T = mg = 2 times 10 = 20 N regardless of the sag angle

quiz_questions.explanation: Each half of the rope pulls at angle θ from horizontal. Vertical equilibrium: 2T sinθ = mg = 20 N. Since θ is small (nearly horizontal), sinθ is small, so T = 20/(2sinθ) >> 10 N. The more horizontal the rope, the greater the tension.

quiz_questions.question_text: A box moves at constant velocity across a rough floor. A student claims that friction is not acting because the box doesn't slow down. Is the student correct?

options.text: Yes the student is correct — if friction were acting on the box, it would immediately decelerate and stop, because friction always causes objects to slow down instantly
No — friction IS acting, but an applied force exactly equals friction. The box has constant velocity because the net force is zero (dynamic equilibrium), not because friction is absent
Yes the student is correct — constant velocity is proof that no friction force is present, since friction would cause the box to decelerate and eventually come to rest
Partially correct — friction only begins acting once the box starts to decelerate, and until that happens there is no friction force present between the surfaces

quiz_questions.explanation: Constant velocity means zero net force (Newton's first law). Friction acts (opposing motion), but the applied force balances it. This is dynamic equilibrium.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 10 kg block sits on a rough incline of angle 25°. μ_s = 0.5. Does the block slide? (g = 10 m/s², tan25° ≈ 0.466)

options.text: Yes it slides because gravity always pulls objects down any incline regardless of friction, since no surface can provide enough friction to hold a 10 kg object
Yes it slides because 10 kg is too heavy for friction to hold on any inclined surface, since heavier objects always overcome static friction and begin sliding
No it stays because blocks never slide on inclines less than 45 degrees, since the critical angle for sliding is always 45 degrees regardless of friction coefficient
No — the block stays put. The component of gravity along the incline = mgsin25° ≈ 42.3 N. The max static friction = μ_s × mgcos25° = 0.5 × 90.6 ≈ 45.3 N. Since 42.3 < 45.3, friction holds the block

quiz_questions.explanation: Down-the-incline force = mgsin25° ≈ 42.3 N. Normal force = mgcos25° ≈ 90.6 N. Max static friction = 0.5 × 90.6 ≈ 45.3 N. Since 42.3 N < 45.3 N, friction is sufficient and the block doesn't slide.
